main_summary.summary: Ben Caldwell, born 1945 in Deming, New Mexico, recounts his trajectory from a multiracial border town shaped by Guadalupe Hidalgo, Pancho Villa, Geronimo, and Pershing-era history through Vietnam, UCLA's L.A. Rebellion film cohort, and four decades of cultural organizing in Leimert Park's Crenshaw district. He describes his projectionist grandfather who navigated Latino, white, and Black-balcony theaters; segregated schooling that forced him into a one-block-misplaced 'all-white' classroom; and the family ethic of pride and self-defense that carried him into the Army and Vietnam, where reading Malcolm X stripped away the religious veil and taught him to be 'a pacifist in a war zone.' At Phoenix College and Arizona State he abandoned football and economics, eventually entering UCLA's MFA program where he joined Haile Gerima and roughly thirteen other students of color in what became the L.A. Rebellion. Caldwell traces the founding of KAOS Network (originally Video 3333) in Leimert Park, his teaching at Howard University with Bernice Johnson Reagon and Alan Lomax, his films Medea, Babylon Is Falling, I & I, and I-Phresh, his Cuban Babalawo and yoga research with Yogi Bhajan, the 1992 Rodney King uprising he witnessed, and ongoing Sankofa-principles hip-hop pedagogy with Project Blowed.
key_themes: KAOS Network as community media laboratory
1992 Los Angeles uprising and CIA-crack allegations
Cross-cultural confluence in art versus politics
main_summary.historical_significance:
Caldwell is one of the founding figures of the L.A. Rebellion, the UCLA-anchored Black independent film movement that produced Haile Gerima, Charles Burnett, Julie Dash, Jamaa Fanaka, and others. His testimony connects three distinct geographies of Black American history that rarely sit in one oral history: the Black-Hispanic Southwest (Calle de los Negros, Calafia, Black Moors, Mimbres), the Vietnam-era draftee experience of seeing oneself as 'the other' chasing 'the other,' and the post-1970s Crenshaw cultural economy where he founded KAOS Network and Project Blowed.

The interview also documents Caldwell's first-person account of the 1992 Rodney King uprising from his Leimert Park location, his collaborations with Worth W. Long, John Outterbridge's Communicative Arts Academy, Shirley Clarke's democratic-media pedagogy, Bernice Johnson Reagon and Alan Lomax (via Bill Barlow's Howard University circle), and the Norton Family Foundation's Phantom Galleries program. His framing of hip-hop as a Sankofa-principles archival remix, and of capoeira / quilombos / Rastafarian resistance as the African diasporic continuum, gives the LoC Civil Rights History Project corpus its strongest cultural-worker / artist-as-organizer testimony for the post-Movement West Coast.

chapters.title: Opening and the Blues at the Smithsonian
chapters.summary: David Cline opens the April 2013 Los Angeles session, and Caldwell recalls teaching at Howard, working with Bernice Johnson Reagon, and talking blues with Alan Lomax and Bill Barlow.

chapters.title: A Grandfather Who Was a Power Broker
chapters.summary: Caldwell traces his family of migrant farmers and his light-skinned grandfather, an uneducated man who bought property, knew the mayor, and worked as a segregated-theater projectionist.

chapters.title: Deming, a Border Crossroads
chapters.summary: He describes Deming, New Mexico as a crossroads of Pancho Villa, Geronimo, Cochise, and the Butterfield Overland Mail, a place of constant influx and Boy Scout stories.

chapters.title: The Colored Baseball Team
chapters.summary: His grandfather ran a barnstorming colored baseball team of Indian, Asian, and Black players across the Southwest, while his father, a train foreman, had reached Marquette.

chapters.title: Storytellers and the Boxcar
chapters.summary: Caldwell recalls storytellers who performed the Signifying Monkey and once killed a rattlesnake mid-tale, and his early life lived partly in a railroad boxcar.

chapters.title: Born in 1945, Around the Movies
chapters.summary: Born in 1945, Caldwell spent his early years with his grandfather and was always around the movie world as his grandfather projected drive-in films.

chapters.title: The Only Black Child in the White School
chapters.summary: By an accident of which side of the railroad track he lived on, Caldwell became the only Black child at the all-white Smith School, was pelted with rocks, then learned to fight and became a jock.

chapters.title: A Proud Family Stands Up
chapters.summary: With about 150 Black migrants in town, Caldwell's father taught his children pride and never to cow-tail to anyone over race, a lesson Caldwell passed on to his siblings.

chapters.title: Cowboys, Police, and a Town's Protection
chapters.topic: Standing Up in a Pioneer Town
chapters.summary: Caldwell recounts his brothers proving themselves to cowboys and his sister fighting a New Mexico cop, after which the hometown judge, knowing the respected family, let them off, an outcome he says would have meant prison or death elsewhere.

chapters.title: Photography, the Desert, and the Draft
chapters.summary: After six months at an Arizona art school, Caldwell was drafted, and on returning began photographing his family and the desert, seeing Black skin against the earth.

chapters.title: Vietnam Strips Away the Veil
chapters.summary: Vietnam pulled the veil from Caldwell's eyes and ended his religiousness when preachers sanctioned killing; he saw shared humanity in the Montagnards, read Malcolm X, and reflected on pride mislabeled as militancy.

chapters.title: Trapped in the Army, Refusing the Knife
chapters.summary: Hating to be trapped, Caldwell refused exploratory knee surgery, was made an ambulatory patient, and was shipped to Japan.

chapters.title: Learning Bureaucracy in Japan
chapters.summary: In Japan he sought out power, eating Thanksgiving with the American ambassador and Christmas with the base general, then used that schooling when shipped back to Vietnam during the Tet Offensive.

chapters.title: A Pacifist in the War Zone
chapters.summary: During Tet, Caldwell's unit pulled an old man in, refused an order to kill him, and walked nearly 150 villagers to safety; he resolved to become a pacifist in a war zone, killing only to survive.

chapters.title: Additive Paint and Phoenix College Football
chapters.summary: Out of the military, Caldwell added paint to his photographs of Black bodies in the desert, work that won him a full ride to UCLA, and recalls proving himself as a fullback at Phoenix College while refusing to be called boy.

chapters.title: Quitting Economics for Revolution
chapters.summary: He abandoned economics when it could not predict the depression it studied, turning to still photography and a revolutionary-philosophy class taught by a defected KGB agent.

chapters.title: The Artist Who Crosses Every Border
chapters.summary: Caldwell argues the artist survives every revolution because art, unlike politics, lets people communicate across borders, a lesson he carried teaching Nazis at CalArts and entering Cuba and Ghana as a spiritual person.

chapters.title: Art as a Political Act
chapters.summary: Pressed on whether art is still political, Caldwell explains he runs his space like television, renting to everyone, even anti-police events, refusing the police's demand for an understanding.

chapters.title: Founding the Community Video Workshop
chapters.summary: Returning to Los Angeles after Howard, Caldwell won a grant as one of the state's first video artists and ran workshops in Shirley Clarke's media-democracy spirit, putting cameras in children's hands.

chapters.title: The Fused Art World of 1970s Watts
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes the holistic Los Angeles art scene he found in 1971 around Watts and John Outterbridge: fused musicians, poets, and painters, African co-ops, drum classes, and the Crenshaw performers that drew him in.

chapters.title: Los Angeles and the History of Forgetting
chapters.summary: Using his ethnographic instinct, Caldwell uncovered Los Angeles as a place colonized by Black Hispanics, the Calle de los Negros, and what Norman Klein called the history of forgetting, which became his banner.

chapters.title: Electronic Cafe and Global Black History
chapters.summary: From the 1984 Electronic Cafe that linked communities through early internet, Caldwell pursued the confluence of cultures and a global Black history of Calafia, Black Moors, the Phoenicians, and Hannibal's Carthage.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: Project Blowed and Hip-Hop as Remix
chapters.summary: Caldwell founded KAOS Network and worked with young people, framing hip-hop as historical remix and homage, introducing kids to James Brown even as they scratched up his albums.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: Filming the History of African People
chapters.summary: He describes his films Babylon Is Falling, Medea, and the chant-driven work compressing the history of African people into minutes, fusing animation, Amiri Baraka's poetry, and a heartbeat into visual ritual.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: I and I, Capoeira, and the Nubian
chapters.summary: Caldwell explains his film I & I on the making of the other, his drive to infuse soul into film's rhythm, capoeira as a coded martial art, and his planned Nubian series tracing the unwritten western migration.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: Against Blaxploitation
chapters.summary: Caldwell distinguishes true stories like Superfly, Shaft, and Sweet Sweetback from the imitations he calls blaxploitation, work that exploits conditions society itself created.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: Music as Cosmology
chapters.summary: He argues that Black music is a hologram of African cosmology seeded by the Egyptians, and that the great migrations followed invasions by figures like Alexander and Caesar.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: The UCLA Cohort and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.summary: As the first film students of color in UCLA's regular program, treated as ethnographic others, Caldwell and about thirteen peers gathered around Haile Gerima, won and lost the grants, and produced some fifty-five films.

chapters.part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
chapters.title: Naming the Rebellion, Embraced in Europe
chapters.summary: Caldwell reflects on the L.A. Rebellion name, the group's stand against blaxploitation, and how their work was celebrated in Europe while shown only in American museums and colleges.

chapters.title: Repurposing Payphones With Sankofa
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes his current Sankofa-principled work repurposing payphones into Wi-Fi totems and computers, developed with a diverse class at the School of Innovation to serve the poor and vulnerable.

chapters.title: Occupying the Virtual Space
chapters.summary: He frames the project as occupying virtual space with Black iconography to resist gentrification, hacking a privately owned payphone in front of his building and meeting LA Metro to help define what they dream toward.

chapters.title: Mapping the Neighborhood's Genius
chapters.summary: Caldwell shows the label and Phoenix logo, QR-coded payphone ideas, and a map of the cultural density of a five-mile radius where nearly every famous Black figure once lived or worked, an economic engine he wants recognized.

chapters.title: Phantom Galleries and Empty Buildings
chapters.summary: He showcases cutting-edge young musicians and religious hip-hop at his space and the phantom-galleries effort that filled nine empty buildings with art and helped upgrade a derelict neighborhood theater.

chapters.title: Cuba, the Babalawos, and an Army of Healers
chapters.summary: In 1999 Caldwell took Black yoga teachers to Cuba to tap cultural memory through the Babalawos, learning of immunization-based body markings studied by physicists and a society that sent healers and 300,000 soldiers against apartheid.

chapters.title: Cuban Music and the Limits of Socialism
chapters.summary: Caldwell recalls Los Munequitos de Matanzas, watching hip-hop age across eighteen years, and the heavy bureaucracy of Cuban socialism that nonetheless guaranteed school, healthcare, and an enriched artistic life.

chapters.title: The Rodney King Verdict and the Uprising
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes the shock of the Rodney King acquittal and the multicultural rage that followed, the anarchy of broad-daylight looting, and his suspicion at how thoroughly the buildings burned.

chapters.title: Saving the Gallery, the Korean Community
chapters.summary: He recounts neighbors saving a Black gallery of African sculpture from fire and the complicated standing of Korean families, whom he saw as the Black people of Asia, tied to Black soldiers from Vietnam.

chapters.title: The Emmett Till Parallel and the Gang Myth
chapters.summary: Drawing on a study, Caldwell compares the uprising's spark to the whisper that killed Emmett Till and argues the gang and crack epidemics were too organized and well armed to be homegrown, pointing to outside agents.

chapters.title: Refusing to Film Degradation
chapters.topic: Art Over the Documentation of Pain
chapters.summary: Caldwell explains he cannot film people in states of degradation, choosing instead works like Word Sound and Power that make flowers from compost, and his lifelong distance from a communism he found bureaucratic and racist, before the closing credit.

loc_video.caption: Ben Caldwell oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Los Angeles, California, 2013 April 11
